questions.prompt: Which power has the role of judging and interpreting the laws?
questions.explanation: The judicial power, exercised by independent judges, settles disputes and interprets the laws. The "constituent power" refers to the authority that drafts or revises the Constitution: it is a trap, because it sounds right but is not one of Montesquieu's three powers.

questions.prompt: In what year did women win the right to vote in France?
questions.explanation: The ordinance of 21 April 1944, issued by General de Gaulle's Provisional Government, granted women the right to vote; they voted for the first time in 1945. France was late: New Zealand had done so as early as 1893. 1958 (the Fifth Republic) and 1968 (May 1968) are decoys drawn from nearby landmark years.

questions.prompt: In a parliamentary regime, which mechanism allows Parliament to bring down the government?
questions.explanation: A vote of no confidence, passed by members of parliament, forces the government to resign: it is the hallmark of the parliamentary regime, where the executive is accountable to Parliament. The veto is a tool of the president (presidential regime), and a shared-initiative referendum serves to propose a law, not to bring down a government.

questions.prompt: What is the current length of the French president's term of office (the quinquennat)?
questions.explanation: Since the referendum of 2000 (under Jacques Chirac), the French presidential term has lasted 5 years: this is the quinquennat, first applied in 2002. Previously it was 7 years (the septennat): "7 years" is therefore the old length, a deliberately credible distractor.

questions.prompt: How many states founded the United Nations in 1945?
questions.explanation: The UN was founded in 1945 by 51 member states, following the San Francisco conference. Do not confuse this with the 193 members today: that is the classic trap. The number 27, for its part, evokes the member states of the European Union — a different organization.

questions.prompt: Which article of the 1789 Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen states that "the principle of all sovereignty resides essentially in the nation"?
questions.explanation: It is Article 3 of the 1789 Declaration that establishes national sovereignty: power emanates from the nation, not from the king. Beware the trap: Article 16, also famous, is the one that requires the separation of powers ("Any society in which... the separation of powers [is] not determined has no Constitution"). Article 1 proclaims that "men are born free and equal".
